cataloging_subject.label: The Owl and the Nightingale (Jesus College MS 29 version)
cataloging_subject.scope_note: The complete 1,794-line Middle English version transmitted in Oxford, Jesus College MS 29 (Part II), ff. 156ra–168vb, represented by the corpus item OwlJ. This witness-specific textual version is distinct from the abstract two-witness work, the collateral Cotton Caligula A IX version, Atkins's 1922 facing-page edition, the OTA and University of Michigan encodings, and the repository XML artifact.

catalog_summary.title: The Owl and the Nightingale (Jesus College MS 29 version)
catalog_summary.author: Anonymous
catalog_summary.date: Composition unresolved, approximately 1175–1285; the surviving Jesus witness was copied by c. 1300
catalog_summary.region: Southern England is the broad conventional localization; the Jesus witness's scribal language is localized to the Ledbury area of Herefordshire

titles.value: The Owl and the Nightingale (Jesus College MS 29 version)

assertion.notes: The parenthetical distinguishes the cataloged Jesus-witness version from the abstract work and the Cotton version.

notes: This conventional modern title belongs to the abstract work and its editions; it is not the Latin rubric written in the Jesus manuscript.

titles.value: The owl and the nightingale (MS Jes. Col. 29)
Incipit altercacio inter filomenam et Bubonem

scope.description: Opening rubric in the Jesus witness

notes: A scribal rubric meaning that the debate between the nightingale and owl begins; it is not treated as an authorial title or one of the poem's 1,794 verse lines.

titles.value: Ich wes in one sumere dale / In one swiþe dyele hale
Ne can ic eu namore telle; / Her nys namore of þisse spelle.

agents.name: J. W. H. Atkins
notes: 1874–1951; editor of the immediate 1922 source edition.

agents.name: Nicholas of Guildford
notes: A candidate author named within the poem and historically credited in cataloging, but the attribution is not proven.

assertion.notes: Nicholas remains a plausible candidate, but the internal references do not prove authorship and alternate theories exist.

scope.description: Underlying 1922 source edition represented by the OTA and U-M OwlJ encodings; the probable 1971 OTA carrier is modeled separately

notes: Atkins edited and transcribed the witness; he is not the medieval author.

OTA combines the date 1922 with New York publisher Russell & Russell, whose matching reprint is dated 1971 by the Library of Congress. Exact electronic carrier dependence remains qualified in the lineage record.

scope.description: Jesus College MS 29 text printed on the odd-numbered facing pages of the 1922 edition

assertion.notes: The normalized span is an intentionally broad discovery range encompassing current competing proposals, not a claim that composition continued across the interval.

notes: The 1190–1210 date inherited by the U-M/OTA headers is one historical position, not settled current consensus.
Proposals include c. 1175–1200, the traditional 1189–1216 or early-thirteenth-century range, and a later-thirteenth-century or post-1272 date.

assertion.notes: Both surviving copies are likely post-1272. This is an attestation boundary, not a composition date or a date assigned to the reconstructed common exemplar.
Southern origin is the broad consensus, but the linguistic and place-name evidence does not securely distinguish Surrey, Dorset, Wessex, the Home Counties, or the south-west Midlands.

notes: This shared localization concerns the poem's reconstructed original dialect and possible composition region; it is distinct from either surviving witness's scribal language.
This broad cultural-origin statement does not identify a precise place of composition.

scope.description: Scribal language of the Jesus College MS 29 version

assertion.notes: This localizes the surviving Jesus witness's scribal language, not necessarily the author's language or place of composition.

form_statements.description: A 1,794-line poem in predominantly octosyllabic rhyming couplets.

assertion.notes: Satiric style is a grounded secondary genre feature; it does not reduce the work's multivalent debate to a single satirical target.
The poem draws on natural science, bestiary traditions, and beast-fable allusions without being reduced to a bestiary entry or beast fable.

scope.description: Closing appeal concerning Nicholas of Guildford, lines 1745–1778

assertion.notes: The poem sends the disputants to Nicholas of Guildford but ends before reporting a judgment.

summaries.value: An owl and a nightingale trade a long sequence of insults, defenses, exempla, and claims about beauty, song, usefulness, morality, love, marriage, religion, natural behavior, and social order. Their contest repeatedly adopts the language and tactics of formal pleading, but neither bird secures a verdict: they agree to take their dispute to Nicholas of Guildford, and the poem ends while they are still on their way. The comic, rhetorically agile irresolution keeps the values represented by both birds in play.

This record describes the complete Jesus College MS 29 textual version represented by OwlJ. The abstract work, its reconstructed lost common exemplar, the Cotton version, modern editions, digital encodings, and manuscript-image objects remain separate related or lineage entities.

The poem comprises 1,794 verse lines and a separate opening Latin rubric. The repository XML's eighteen supplied 100-line DIV1 groups (the last holding 94 lines), odd-page PB markers, and folio/column locators are encoding and edition navigation, not intrinsic medieval sections. Five expected old-foliation column labels survive as literal text within L elements rather than as MILESTONE elements; together with 47 MILESTONE elements they cover the legacy sequence 229r.1–241v.2.

extent.description: One complete verse work comprising 1,794 lines, conventionally 897 rhyming couplets. The repository BODY contains 1,794 nonempty L elements for poem lines, plus a separate Latin HEAD rubric, but no LG elements; 897 is therefore an intellectual couplet count supported by the edition and DIMEV, not an XML wrapper count. L/@N appears only at every fifth canonical line and ends at 1790, so line extent is positional rather than a count of consecutively numbered attributes. The 18 supplied DIV1 wrappers and 47 MILESTONE elements are representation metrics; five further legacy folio-column labels are literal line text and are not physical gaps.

content_parts.label: The Owl and the Nightingale (Jesus College MS 29 version)
content_parts.summary: The complete Jesus-witness poem from its opening summer-valley scene through the disputants' unresolved journey toward Nicholas of Guildford.

locators.value: BODY HEAD rubric followed by 1,794 positional L elements; 18 supplied DIV1 groups

notes: The current foliation is used for manuscript citation; legacy foliation is retained as a crosswalk because it appears in Atkins, OTA, and the XML.

related_works.title: The Owl and the Nightingale
notes: The abstract work survives in two complete but textually distinct witnesses copied independently from a lost common exemplar.

evidence.citation: General Corpus, CME/source/CME_phase_1-2/OwlJ.xml, pinned Kyvero-Press/CME commit e1d77c88ca561be924b2fedc54dd474be2e7e49c.
evidence.locator: HEADER; BODY opening HEAD; all DIV1, L, PB, MILESTONE, and TRAILER elements
evidence.summary: Identifies OwlJ as an anonymous Jesus College MS 29 text drawn from Atkins 1922; preserves the Latin rubric, complete poem text, supplied 100-line groups, print-page markers, legacy folio-column locators, and explicit.

evidence.citation: Local structural audit of the pinned OwlJ XML with lxml/XPath, 15 July 2026.
evidence.summary: Counts exactly 1,794 nonempty BODY L nodes, 18 supplied DIV1 groups, 19 HEAD elements, 75 PB elements, 47 MILESTONE elements, and five literal folio-column labels embedded in poem L text. Groups 1–17 contain 100 L nodes and group 18 contains 94. L/@N marks every fifth line through 1790 rather than every line.
notes: The Latin rubric is a BODY HEAD, not a poem L node. Literal locator strings were retained in transcription metrics and not interpreted as missing physical text.

evidence.citation: Local comparison of current OTA OwlJ SGML, current MEDictionary OwlJ XML, and pinned repository OwlJ XML, 15 July 2026.
evidence.locator: complete files; opening rubric; folio/column boundaries; all poetic lines
evidence.summary: After resolving OTA thorn entities and ignoring whitespace, the OTA rubric plus 1,794 poetic lines matches the U-M/CME rubric and poetic text exactly. OTA serializes the rubric as the first of 1,795 L tags; both current MEDictionary XML and pinned CME XML promote it to HEAD and retain 1,794 poem L nodes. All three preserve only 47 structural folio locators plus the same five literal locator strings, showing that the locator defect predates the current U-M and repository wrappers.
notes: The current MEDictionary file also adds print-page and supplied-group navigation; the pinned repository file has a distinct wrapper variant with NODE and EPB markup. These structural differences do not alter the 1,794 poetic line strings.

evidence.citation: University of Michigan Library, Corpus of Middle English Prose and Verse, OwlJ, The owl and the nightingale (MS Jes. Col. 29).
evidence.summary: Current item page for the complete public web text; identifies the title, author, Atkins source, Jesus-only extraction, and current public-domain/NoC-US access statement.

evidence.citation: Parsed Corpus of Middle English Poetry, text record ‘The Owl and the Nightingale.’
evidence.locator: Genre/subjects; dialect of original composition; date; versification; identifiers; edition and manuscript-base fields
evidence.summary: Provides a documented synthesis of the poem as an animal debate, the broad Southern localization and its limitations, the unresolved 1175–1285 date range, 897 couplets and 1,794 lines, DIMEV/IMEV identifiers, Atkins source edition, both witnesses, and the distinction between original and manuscript dialects.

evidence.citation: Susanna Fein, ed. and trans., The Owl and the Nightingale and the English Poems of Oxford, Jesus College, MS 29 (II), Introduction (Middle English Texts Series, 2022; digital edition 2024).
evidence.locator: The Owl and the Nightingale introduction and edition note
evidence.summary: Current scholarly account of the poem's 1,794-line couplet form, two-witness transmission from a lost common exemplar, Jesus and Cotton loci, Jesus scribal adaptation, uncertain dating and authorship, rhetoric, law, orality, sexuality, music, natural lore, and deliberately unresolved debate.

evidence.citation: Linne R. Mooney, Daniel W. Mosser, Ian Cornelius, and Michael Johnston, Digital Index of Middle English Verse, version 2.0-alpha.1 (2026), record 2307.
evidence.summary: The current official prerelease records the conventional title, incipit, explicit, 1,794 lines in couplets, NIMEV 1384, subjects debates/birds/animals/birds as narrators, Jesus Part II ff. 156–168v, Cotton ff. 233ra–246ra, and Ker 1963 facsimile references.
notes: Version 2.0-alpha.1 is explicitly a prerelease. Its abbreviated Jesus locus is refined here from the exact current catalog/facsimile and Fein to ff. 156ra–168vb.

evidence.citation: Bodleian Libraries, Catalogue of Western Medieval Manuscripts in Oxford Libraries, Jesus College MS 29.
evidence.locator: Part 2 and item 3, The Owl and the Nightingale
evidence.summary: Describes the trilingual anthology as Part II, ?1272 × 1290s, and catalogs the poem and manuscript context, holding, foliation, language, and physical object.

evidence.citation: Jesus College Oxford and Digital Bodleian, complete digital facsimile of Jesus College MS 29.
evidence.summary: The complete-codex images verify the current-locus mapping, opening red Latin rubric, two-column layout, and the old pencil foliation beginning 229 on current f. 156r.

evidence.citation: British Library Archives and Manuscripts Catalogue, Cotton MS Caligula A IX.
evidence.locator: ff. 233r–246r, The Owl and the Nightingale
evidence.summary: Catalogs the collateral Cotton witness, composite-codex date, English origin, physical description, and exact folio locus.

The complete 1922 edition confirms Atkins's title-page responsibility, independent examination of both witnesses, facing-page Jesus and Cotton texts, stated emendation practice, and the Jesus text's odd-page range. The lineage record separately models the probable 1971 Russell & Russell carrier indicated by OTA's otherwise inconsistent publisher/date statement.

open_questions.question: Which proposed chronology best explains the poem's historical and textual evidence: the traditional 1189–1216 range or a later-thirteenth-century/post-1272 composition?
next_steps: Consult the full arguments and bibliography in Fein 2022, especially the competing historical allusions.
Treat 1175–1285 as a broad discovery envelope rather than a settled date, and seek evidence that can narrow the competing proposals.
Do not elevate the U-M/OTA 1190–1210 header over later alternatives without resolving the current scholarly disagreement.

open_questions.question: Can the poem's references to Nicholas of Guildford support an authorial attribution, or is anonymous authorship the only defensible cataloging position?
next_steps: Retain Nicholas only as a disputed attributed author unless new external evidence resolves the internal-reference problem.
Review competing authorship theories without turning the protagonist's proposed judge into the author by default.

open_questions.question: Can the poem's place of composition be localized more narrowly than Southern England without conflating authorial origin with the Jesus witness's Ledbury-area scribal dialect?
next_steps: Consult current dialectological and textual scholarship for evidence independent of either surviving scribe.
Keep catalog claims such as a probable Surrey or Dorset origin attributed to their sources until reconciled with the broader scholarship.

notes: The record deliberately catalogs the Jesus-witness version because OwlJ extracts that witness alone from Atkins's facing-page edition; OwlC is the separately encoded Cotton counterpart.
The reconstructed common exemplar X is a textual hypothesis shared by the two witnesses, not the authorial original and not an extant manuscript.
Current foliation is used for direct manuscript citation. Atkins/OTA/XML legacy foliation is retained as an explicit crosswalk because current f. 156r bears the old pencil number 229.
The source XML's profile date 1190–1210 and the former Nicholas attribution are recorded as historical claims rather than silently promoted to current consensus.
